paper_metadata.title: Perceived fairness and public acceptability of carbon pricing: A review of the literature
paper_metadata.authors: Sara Maestre-Andrés, Stefan Drews, Jeroen van den Bergh
paper_metadata.geographic_scope: Primarily North America, Europe, and Australia; a few studies from India and South Africa.
paper_metadata.empirical_scope_or_sample: Review of 43 empirical studies on carbon taxes, ecological tax reform, and a few cap-and-trade studies.
paper_metadata.carbon_tax_relevance_summary: Systematic review of empirical evidence on fairness perceptions, acceptability, and revenue recycling for carbon taxes and related fuel taxes; tax-specific support effects are synthesized from primary studies.

determinants.raw_name_in_paper: Higher energy prices and less purchasing power
determinants.definition: Perceived increase in energy bills and reduction in disposable income from the tax.
determinants.mechanism: Higher costs make the tax feel personally costly and unfair, lowering support.
determinants.exact_tax_policy_context: Carbon-tax acceptability and support in reviewed studies.
determinants.evidence_quote: higher energy prices and less purchasing power decreased acceptability of carbon pricing
determinants.statistical_or_result_detail: 13 studies mentioned; 8 significant negative findings in Table 2.

determinants.raw_name_in_paper: Loss of jobs
determinants.definition: Belief that the tax will reduce employment.
determinants.mechanism: Expected labor-market harm increases opposition to the tax.
determinants.exact_tax_policy_context: Carbon-tax and fuel-tax acceptability in reviewed studies.
determinants.evidence_quote: potential loss of jobs due to carbon pricing influenced negatively
determinants.statistical_or_result_detail: 5 studies mentioned; 1 significant negative and 2 non-significant findings in Table 2.

determinants.raw_name_in_paper: Policy does not limit freedom of choice
determinants.definition: Belief that the tax leaves individual choice unconstrained.
determinants.mechanism: Less coercive policy is seen as more legitimate and acceptable.
determinants.exact_tax_policy_context: Carbon-tax acceptability in reviewed studies.
determinants.evidence_quote: policy does not limit freedom of choice

determinants.raw_name_in_paper: Neutral cost distribution (all citizens pay the same share of income)
determinants.definition: Perception that the tax burden is proportional to income.
determinants.mechanism: Fairer burden sharing reduces distributive concerns and raises support.
determinants.exact_tax_policy_context: Carbon-tax acceptability in reviewed studies.
determinants.evidence_quote: all citizens pay the same share of income

determinants.raw_name_in_paper: Regressive incidence (all citizens pay the same amount; effects on the poor)
determinants.definition: Perception that lower-income households bear a larger burden than richer households.
determinants.mechanism: Unequal burden on the poor lowers perceived fairness and support.
determinants.exact_tax_policy_context: Carbon-tax acceptability in reviewed studies.
determinants.evidence_quote: regressive incidence was found to influence its rejection

determinants.raw_name_in_paper: Progressive incidence (higher-income citizens pay a larger share of income)
determinants.definition: Perception that richer households bear a larger share of the tax burden.
determinants.mechanism: Burden shifting toward higher-income groups increases distributive fairness and support.
determinants.exact_tax_policy_context: Carbon-tax acceptability in reviewed studies.
determinants.evidence_quote: progressive distribution of the costs of the instrument determined people’s acceptability

determinants.raw_name_in_paper: Exemptions and privileges given to some sectors
determinants.definition: Perception that some sectors are unfairly exempted or favored.
determinants.mechanism: Special treatment undermines perceived equal treatment and legitimacy.
determinants.exact_tax_policy_context: Carbon-tax acceptability in reviewed studies.
determinants.evidence_quote: exemptions and privileges given to some sectors
determinants.statistical_or_result_detail: 4 studies mentioned; 1 positive negative effect in Table 2.

determinants.raw_name_in_paper: Satisfaction with governmental information provision about the policy
determinants.definition: Satisfaction with how government explains the tax and its use of revenues.
determinants.mechanism: Better information reduces uncertainty and improves acceptability.
determinants.exact_tax_policy_context: Carbon-tax acceptability in reviewed studies.
determinants.evidence_quote: policy acceptability increases when people are satisfied with governmental information provision

determinants.raw_name_in_paper: Trust in government
determinants.definition: Belief that government will implement the tax and use revenues as promised.
determinants.mechanism: Institutional trust lowers suspicion about misuse and raises support.
determinants.exact_tax_policy_context: Carbon-tax and CO2-tax acceptability in reviewed studies.
determinants.evidence_quote: Trust in the government was reported ... as increasing the probability of acceptability of carbon pricing

determinants.raw_name_in_paper: Limited understanding of the policy
determinants.definition: Poor understanding of how the tax works or why it is used.
determinants.mechanism: Confusion makes the tax harder to justify and less acceptable.
determinants.exact_tax_policy_context: Carbon-tax acceptability in reviewed studies.
determinants.evidence_quote: limited understanding of the policy
determinants.statistical_or_result_detail: 1 qualitative study reported a negative effect.

determinants.raw_name_in_paper: Overall fairness
determinants.definition: Overall judgment that the carbon tax is fair after weighing its impacts and design.
determinants.mechanism: A global fairness judgment acts as a heuristic that boosts support.
determinants.exact_tax_policy_context: Carbon-tax acceptability in reviewed studies.
determinants.evidence_quote: perceived overall fairness positively affects public acceptability of carbon pricing

determinants.raw_name_in_paper: Environmental projects
determinants.definition: Using carbon-tax revenue for environmental projects such as renewables or climate programs.
determinants.mechanism: Earmarking may increase legitimacy, but preferences depend on project type.
determinants.exact_tax_policy_context: Carbon-tax revenue recycling and earmarking.
determinants.evidence_quote: Earmarking for environmental projects ... affected positively people’s acceptability

determinants.raw_name_in_paper: Funding technological innovation and energy efficiency
determinants.definition: Using tax revenue for technology grants, innovation, or efficiency improvements.
determinants.mechanism: Visible climate-related investment can increase perceived usefulness and support.
determinants.exact_tax_policy_context: Carbon-tax revenue recycling to innovation and efficiency.
determinants.evidence_quote: Funding technological innovation and energy efficiency was found to positively affect people’s policy acceptability

determinants.raw_name_in_paper: Redistributing revenue to vulnerable groups (low income households, elderly)
determinants.definition: Returning revenue to low-income households or other vulnerable groups.
determinants.mechanism: Targeted compensation offsets regressivity and improves distributive fairness.
determinants.exact_tax_policy_context: Carbon-tax revenue recycling to vulnerable groups.
determinants.evidence_quote: redistributing revenue to vulnerable groups ... increased people’s acceptability

determinants.raw_name_in_paper: Equal share of revenue given to each taxpayer
determinants.definition: Returning the same revenue amount to all taxpayers.
determinants.mechanism: Universal rebates can offset burden and appear fairer than keeping revenues.
determinants.exact_tax_policy_context: Carbon-tax revenue recycling via equal per-capita transfers.
determinants.evidence_quote: Equal share of revenue given to each taxpayer ... increased people’s acceptability

determinants.raw_name_in_paper: Environmental tax reform (reducing existing taxes)
determinants.definition: Using carbon-tax revenues to cut existing taxes such as labor or income taxes.
determinants.mechanism: Support depends on trust, perceived double dividend, and whether the shift seems credible.
determinants.exact_tax_policy_context: Carbon-tax revenue recycling via reductions in other taxes.
determinants.evidence_quote: an environmental tax reform (reducing existing taxes) was found to affect negatively the acceptability

determinants.raw_name_in_paper: Tax rebate
determinants.definition: Returning revenue to taxpayers through a rebate.
determinants.mechanism: Rebates can be seen as fair compensation, but targeted rebates may be resisted.
determinants.exact_tax_policy_context: Carbon-tax revenue recycling via tax rebates.
determinants.evidence_quote: Using the revenues for refunding citizens through a tax rebate showed contradictory results

determinants.raw_name_in_paper: Funding public transportation
determinants.definition: Using revenue to finance public transport.
determinants.mechanism: Improving low-carbon alternatives can make the tax more acceptable.
determinants.exact_tax_policy_context: Carbon-tax revenue recycling to public transportation.
determinants.evidence_quote: Funding public transportation positively influenced people’s acceptability of carbon pricing

determinants.raw_name_in_paper: Mitigate negative impacts of climate change
determinants.definition: Using revenues for projects that reduce climate damages or emissions.
determinants.mechanism: Climate-related earmarking can raise perceived policy purpose and support.
determinants.exact_tax_policy_context: Carbon-tax revenue recycling to climate-mitigation purposes.
determinants.evidence_quote: Mitigate negative impacts of climate change

determinants.raw_name_in_paper: General budget
determinants.definition: Letting carbon-tax revenue go to the general government budget.
determinants.mechanism: Non-earmarked revenues may look less credible and less fairly targeted.
determinants.exact_tax_policy_context: Carbon-tax revenues sent to the general budget.
determinants.evidence_quote: revenues going into the general budget

exclusions_or_uncertainties.issue: Mixed instrument evidence in the source review
exclusions_or_uncertainties.reason: Several reviewed studies also covered cap-and-trade; only tax-specific acceptability findings were extracted here.
